name: Hypocalcaemia
identifyBy: Prolonged QT interval (>470ms in women, >450ms in men)
Prolongation is due to ST segment lengthening, NOT T wave changes
T wave shape is relatively normal (unlike other long QT causes)
QTc correlates inversely with serum calcium level
Tetany, paraesthesia, Chvostek/Trousseau signs in severe cases

measurements.stSegment: Flat and prolonged - ST segment duration increased

management: Check serum calcium (ionised if possible), magnesium, phosphate, PTH
IV Calcium gluconate 10–20mL of 10% over 10 min (monitor ECG during infusion)
Severe (tetany, seizure, QTc >500ms): IV bolus, then infusion (10 ampoules in 500mL over 24 hours)
Oral calcium + Vitamin D for chronic management
Identify cause: hypoparathyroidism, pancreatitis, rhabdomyolysis, massive transfusion, bisphosphonates
pearls: The prolonged QT in hypocalcaemia is due to ST lengthening, not T wave shape change - unlike drug-induced long QT
Magnesium and calcium derangements often co-exist - replace both
Risk of VT/Torsades is lower in hypocalcaemia than drug-induced long QT despite similar QTc
Burns victims and pancreatitis patients are at high risk for hypocalcaemia
IV calcium in suspected digoxin toxicity: CAUTION - same risk of stone heart syndrome
differentials: Drug-induced long QT (T wave shape changes, biphasic T waves)
Congenital long QT syndrome (T wave notching, family history)

qaNotes: Electrolyte pattern emphasises QT/ST duration and T-wave morphology.
